paper_analysis.summary: This paper describes the refractiveindex.info database, an open-source repository of optical constants for many materials, including linear optical constants (n, k) and nonlinear refractive index (n2). The dataset is a curated compilation from peer-reviewed literature, manufacturer datasheets, and authoritative books, stored in standardized YAML files with metadata and organized through catalog files and a library-like hierarchy for browsing and comparison.

figures.description: Screenshot of the n2explorer.py graphical user interface showing visual navigation and comparison of nonlinear refractive index data for SiO2 from multiple publications; the text states nkexplorer.py provides a similar interface for linear optical constants.
figures.likely_source: processed data assembled from literature sources

paper_analysis.raw_measurement_details: The paper does not report new experimental raw measurements. It explicitly states that it does not generate original experimental data, but instead compiles existing optical constants from published sources and manufacturer datasheets.

dataset_characterization.data_availability_statement: The dataset is available at Figshare, and the codebase is publicly available on GitHub under CC0. The database can also be browsed via the RefractiveIndex.INFO website. The paper states that as of December 2023 the dataset contains 3135 linear-optics records on 605 materials and 193 nonlinear-optics records on 89 materials.
dataset_characterization.raw_data_description: No raw experimental files are provided by this paper. The underlying source materials are external publications, datasheets, books, and sometimes author-provided original data; when only graphical data exist, plots may be digitized as a last resort.
dataset_characterization.processed_data_description: The core dataset consists of human-readable YAML files, each representing one material under specific conditions and tied to a particular publication. Records contain REFERENCES, DATA, optional COMMENTS, and SPECS. DATA may be a dispersion formula, tabulated n/k values, combined n and k tables, or tabulated n2 values. Wavelength is always in micrometers; n2 is in m^2/W.
dataset_characterization.figure_data_description: No figure-specific source-data package is described. Figure 1 is a screenshot of the n2explorer GUI and appears to be illustrative rather than a data figure with downloadable source data.
dataset_characterization.scripts_description: Yes. The paper explicitly mentions Python scripts used to generate plots for validation, scripts for converting models into tabulated records, a dedicated script for converting Zemax AGF glass catalogs, and two GUI tools: nkexplorer.py and n2explorer.py. It also mentions third-party scripts on GitHub.
dataset_characterization.notes: The dataset is not raw measurement output; it is a curated, standardized compilation. Data extraction is mostly manual. Sources can be dispersion formulas, tabulated numerical data, models converted by scripts, graphical-only plots digitized when necessary, and glass catalogs automatically converted. The YAML schema distinguishes mandatory citation and data fields from optional metadata fields. The catalog files define a hierarchical 'library' structure with SHELF, BOOK, PAGE, DIVIDER, and info entries pointing to YAML records and HTML info pages.

classification_prior.raw_data_evidence: The paper says, 'our work does not generate original experimental data' and focuses on compiling existing optical constants.
classification_prior.source_data_evidence: The dataset is a repository of optical constants compiled from publications and datasheets, stored as YAML records and accompanied by scripts for plotting, conversion, and browsing.
classification_prior.data_availability_section_relevant: “The dataset is available at Figshare.”; “The code that underpins the refractiveindex.info database is made accessible under the Creative Commons Zero (CC0) license.”; “users can create alternative ‘catalog’ files tailored to their specific needs.”; “two Python scripts, nkexplorer.py and n2explorer.py, are housed in the tools folder.”
